text: Robotic treatment of atypical GERD symptoms with combined gastric diverticulectomy and Nissen fundoplication. The patient is a seventy-one-year-old female who presented to the office with persistent cough and had been treated for GERD with a PPI. She had had recurrent issues with pneumonia, upper respiratory infections, and episodes of coughing of small chunks of food. She had been repeatedly treated with steroids, however, she continues to have recurrent episodes and no improvement in her respiratory symptoms. As a part of her workup, the patient underwent upper endoscopy. This showed signs of esophagitis as well as reflux as well as hiatal hernia and a gastric diverticulum. An upper GI double contrast study confirmed these findings which showed gastroesophageal reflux as well as a posterior gastric outpouch measuring approximately seven centimeters in size. As a result of these findings, it was thought the patient would benefit from a robotic intervention that would include hiatal hernia repair, nissen fundoplication, as well as gastric diverticulectomy.

The surgeon uses robotic hook cautery to dissect the gastrohepatic ligament, with one robotic arm serving as a temporary liver retractor for improved access to the hiatus. After the gastrohepatic ligament is fully dissected, the gastroesophageal membrane is visible, with the right crus identified to the left.

The surgeon dissects anteriorly to expose the esophagus using robotic instruments. The short gastric vessels are taken down with a robotic vessel sealer, allowing further mobilization of the stomach. The spleen is visible in the background. The esophagus is further mobilized, focusing on the left crux.

The surgeon visualizes the gastric diverticulum after complete mobilization of the stomach and esophagus. Dissection continues to delineate the borders of the diverticulum, with a Penrose drain aiding manipulation at the gastroesophageal junction. The esophagus is further mobilized into the mediastinum. Upper endoscopy confirms the presence and base of the gastric diverticulum for resection. Two fires of a robotic 60mm stapler are used to resect the diverticulum, followed by endoscopic confirmation of an intact staple line and complete resection.

The surgeon closes a small hiatal hernia using an O non-absorbable barb suture. Following the closure, the Nissen fundoplication is completed by suturing the anterior gastric wall to the anterior surface of the esophagus, approximating these structures.
The surgeon passes the final U stitch to complete the Nissen fundoplication wrap.
